title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marketing expert called Abbi Grant talking about the techniques used by luxury brands to attract customers. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marketing expert called Abbi Grant talking about the techniques used by luxury brands to attract customers. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Abbi Grant discusses the emotional and psychological triggers behind high-end consumer behavior.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Abbi. Why would someone pay ten times more for a watch that tells the same time as a basic model? As a luxury brand consultant, that’s the question I answer every day. My fascination with consumer psychology didn't start in a boardroom; it actually began when I observed a [7]long queue[/7] outside a designer store in Paris during a rainstorm. I was amazed that people were willing to endure physical discomfort just for the chance to buy a handbag. I realized then that luxury isn't about utility; it’s about identity.

The most effective tool in luxury marketing is 'scarcity'. If everyone can have it, it’s not luxury. I’ve found that the [8]waiting list[/8] is a brand’s most powerful psychological weapon. By making a customer wait six months for a product, the brand increases its perceived value. It creates a sense of achievement when the item is finally acquired. It’s a deliberate strategy to ensure the brand remains aspirational rather than accessible.

We also focus heavily on 'heritage'. Luxury brands love to tell stories about their founders. I’ve noticed that [9]black-and-white photos[/9] of traditional workshops are a key part of modern advertising campaigns. They suggest a connection to a slower, more authentic time, even if the product is actually made using state-of-the-art technology. The customer isn't just buying an object; they are buying a piece of history.

The retail environment is equally important. When you walk into a luxury boutique, it shouldn't feel like a shop; it should feel like a gallery. I’ve noticed that the [10]lighting design[/10] is the most critical element in creating this atmosphere. It’s often dim and focused, highlighting individual products like pieces of art. This slows the customer down, making them feel like they are in a special, protected space away from the noise of the street.

Digital marketing has presented a new challenge. How do you maintain exclusivity online? I found that [11]private members' portals[/11] are the best way to handle this. By requiring a password or an invitation to view certain collections, brands can replicate the 'hidden' feel of a high-end showroom on a global scale. It allows them to use the reach of the internet without damaging their elite status.

Sustainability is the latest trend in luxury. Customers now want to know that their expensive items aren't harming the planet. I’ve found that the [12]traceability of materials[/12] is now the top priority for high-net-worth individuals. They want to know exactly which farm their leather came from or which mine produced their diamonds. Transparency is becoming the new definition of luxury.

Looking forward, I believe that 'experiential luxury' will overtake physical goods. I’m worried that the [13]market saturation[/13] of luxury logos will make physical items less desirable to the truly wealthy. People are starting to value unique experiences—like a private concert or a trip to space—over another designer watch. The symbol of status is shifting from what you own to what you’ve done.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]guest column[/14] for a major business magazine about the future of the luxury sector. I want to help traditional brands adapt to a younger, more socially-conscious audience. Thank you for your time.
